title: Building a framed ledged and braced garden gate
subtitle: Rails and braces on the back, boards on the face, hung on two hinges
excerpt: How to build a simple framed ledged and braced gate from sawn softwood and featheredge or tongue-and-groove boards: sizing the frame, cutting the cross rails and diagonal brace, assembling, fitting the boards, and hanging on a timber post.
sourceNotes: Newey and Drage, Practical Carpentry and Joinery (c. 1905). Collins Complete DIY Manual.

content.text: A ledged and braced gate is the simplest old-style gate. Horizontal
ledges
on the back, a diagonal
brace

to stop it sagging, and boards fixed across the front. The brace direction is the part most builders get wrong. The brace must rise from the hinge corner up to the latch corner. That way it is in compression when the gate hangs and carries its own weight.
Materials
Sawn softwood for ledges, 75 x 38 mm.
Three ledges for a standard height gate: top, bottom, and mid-height.
Sawn softwood for brace, 75 x 38 mm.
Cut to span diagonally from the lower hinge corner to the upper latch corner.
Featheredge boards or rough-sawn tongue-and-groove boards.
Width to suit; allow for gaps between boards (featheredge boards are overlapped; tongue-and-groove boards fit tight).
75 mm round-wire nails or 65 mm wood screws.
T-strap hinges, 300 mm, galvanised, and latch.
Two hinges minimum for a garden gate.
Preservative or primer for the timber.
Measuring and cutting
Step 1, measure the opening.

Measure the clear width of the gate opening and subtract 25 mm for swing clearance on the latch side and 10 mm on the hinge side. This is the finished gate width. Measure the height and subtract 10 mm for ground clearance.
Step 2, cut the ledges and brace.

Cut three ledge pieces to the full gate width. Cut the brace: lay it across the diagonal from the lower hinge corner to the upper latch corner on the assembled ledges and mark the compound bevel angle for each end cut. Cut both ends. The brace should fit snugly between the inner faces of the top and bottom ledges at the correct diagonal angle.
Assembly
Step 3, cut and lay the face boards.

Cut all face boards to the gate height. Lay them face down on a flat surface. Lay the three ledges across them at the correct positions (top ledge 100 mm from top, bottom ledge 100 mm from bottom, mid ledge centred). Check the assembly is square by measuring the two diagonals; they must be equal.
Step 4, fix the boards to the ledges.

Nail or screw each board to each ledge. Two fixings per board per ledge. For nailing, use round-wire nails driven at a slight angle and clenched (bent over) on the back of the ledge for extra holding.
Step 5, fit the brace.
Fit the brace into position between the ledges. Fix it to each ledge with two screws from the face through the boards and into the brace end.
Hanging the gate
Step 6, apply preservative and fit the hinges.

Apply a coat of wood preservative or primer to all timber surfaces before hanging. Position the two T-strap hinges on the gate (one at the top ledge, one at the bottom ledge). Fix the T-plate to the gate face. Hold the gate in the opening at the correct height and mark the hinge pin positions on the post. Fix the post-plate of each hinge to the post. Test that the gate opens and closes freely.
Troubleshooter

attrs.fix: Fit a new brace running from the lower hinge corner to the upper latch corner. Remove the incorrectly directed brace.
attrs.cause: Brace running the wrong direction (from upper hinge to lower latch, which puts it in tension rather than compression under the gate's own weight).
attrs.symptom: Gate sags on the latch side over time

attrs.fix: Apply a full coat of preservative and paint to all surfaces including end grain. For replacement: use seasoned or pressure-treated timber.
attrs.cause: Unseasoned or undried timber used, or the boards were not pre-treated before assembly.
attrs.symptom: Gate warps after the first winter
